video_title: Samir Patel, Global Head of Global Market Sales, Nomura Securities
gist: Nomura's global head of markets sales Samir Patel details how a non-money-center dealer competes by focusing on niche edges (the activist/corporate share-accumulation business, Japan access, QIS), a 2021 shift from regional to global-product management, and a sales-trading alignment regime built on credible model P&L. He flags rising junior attrition out of the industry entirely and the growing dominance of non-bank liquidity providers as the key structural threats.

themes.theme: Niche-Edge Competitive Strategy for a Non-Money-Center Dealer

insights.claim: Samir Patel argues that as a non-money-center bank lacking large deposits, cheap funding, and balance-sheet scale, Nomura must deliberately pick narrow areas of competitive advantage rather than try to be a one-stop counterparty across all products.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Defines the structural disadvantage of non-money-center dealers and the only viable counter-strategy: pick spots, which shapes where Nomura will and won't compete for flow.
insights.quote: we're not a moneys center bank. So we don't have large deposits. Our cost of funding we don't have the scale of balance sheet and the cheap cost of funding that some of our competitors.

insights.claim: Patel says Nomura built a share-accumulation business from scratch over ~15 years that lets clients build large concentrated stakes in public securities under strict confidentiality, and claims Nomura is by far the leading provider of these services.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: A concrete, defensible niche where a smaller dealer dominates by leveraging private-side infrastructure rather than balance sheet, expanding the buyer pool for concentrated public-equity stakes.
insights.quote: we built the business from scratch we built in the private side of the business. We have systems and infrastructure to keep utmost confidentiality.

insights.claim: Patel notes the accumulation business that began serving activist clients has expanded to corporates, sponsors, hedge funds, and asset managers, and uses both delta-one and option structures.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Signals a broad and growing demand for confidential stake-building tools across investor types, relevant to anyone tracking activist or strategic-stake flows.
insights.quote: We now have corporate clients we have sponsor clients other hedge funds and asset managers that are regularly using this type of business.

insights.claim: Patel states Nomura participates in commodities only in a much more targeted way, concentrated in precious metals, unlike its broad coverage across rates, FX, securitized products, credit, and equities.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Maps where Nomura is and isn't a competitive counterparty, useful for clients allocating commodity flow.
insights.quote: it's largely similar to the biggest competitors in the space with the exception of commodities where we're much more targeted in in just precious metals at the moment.

insights.claim: Patel says Nomura switched roughly five years ago from a purely regional management structure (Americas, Europe, Asia ex-Japan, Japan) to a global product-aligned structure with named global product heads and a global sales head role.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Explains the management reorganization that underpins Nomura's recent cross-selling and market-share gains; a template for matrixed sell-side firms.
insights.quote: 5 years ago we changed that structure so it was more along global product alignments. So we named uh global product heads for equities, rates, FX etc.

insights.claim: Patel reports that before the reorg there was little dialogue between regional fixed-income and equities sales heads, whereas now there is regular cross-product dialogue and a 'respect the matrix' culture giving product and region equal input.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Illustrates how breaking down regional silos converts a fragmented franchise into a global one that can capture more client wallet.
insights.quote: prior to the five years ago you know there was very little dialogue um amongst I would say my counterparts at the time

insights.claim: Patel says Nomura invested heavily in a senior relationship management (SRM) function so its largest clients have a single internal point of contact mirroring the client's own top-of-house function.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Shows how dealers institutionalize top-of-house relationships to defend and grow share with the biggest accounts.
insights.quote: we have that one-stop service that can internally uh understand kind of the different issues or opportunities and then uh interface with our biggest clients

themes.theme: Client Profitability Measurement and Wallet Capture

insights.claim: Patel describes a 'model P&L' tool that measures each client's profitability from trade inception to maturity, intended to direct balance sheet and resources toward the most profitable clients as data accumulates.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Quantitative client-profitability scoring increasingly governs how dealers allocate scarce balance sheet and RWA, affecting which clients get pricing and capital.
insights.quote: measuring in our flow businesses in particular client profitability and it helps measure just the client profitability from day one to maturity

insights.claim: Patel frames the dealer-client relationship as a holistic 'balance of trade' that nets revenue against all resources extended including capital, research, counterparty RWA, salespeople, and operational/onboarding support.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Buy-side desks should expect dealers to score the full resource bundle, not just commissions, when deciding pricing and capacity.
insights.quote: is this a good balance of trade for both parties

insights.claim: Patel says the next ambition is to use AI to capture data on trades Nomura lost or missed, widening the model-P&L dataset beyond only trades won.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Capturing lost-trade data would sharpen competitive pricing and reveal where a dealer is uncompetitive, a meaningful edge if executed.
insights.quote: what I would love to do is capture you know more of the trades that we missed and so using AI tools so we're capturing even a wider subset of data

themes.theme: Japan Access as a Structural Edge and Cross-Market Linkages

insights.claim: Patel observes that Japanese equity markets at all-time highs plus government-driven shareholder-return reforms have drawn international clients to allocate more capital to Japan, while the move in JGB rates and yields has made Japan compelling for macro investors.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Confirms sustained foreign inflows into Japanese equities and rates, where Nomura's home-market franchise gives it differentiated liquidity and color.
insights.quote: The equity markets are at all-time highs. There's been a lot of, I would say, government reform and support to kind of improve shareholder returns. So, we've seen a lot of international clients allocating more capital there.

insights.claim: Curnutt recalls that the August 5, 2024 surge in the Japanese yen cascaded into Japanese rates and sent the VIX into the 50s for roughly five hours, illustrating how a shock in one region propagates across products and geographies.
insights.speaker: Dean Curnutt
insights.so_what: A reminder that yen carry and Japan rate shocks transmit rapidly into global equity volatility, demanding cross-asset risk monitoring.
insights.quote: you had this giant move in the Japanese yen and it set off this cascading impact uh in rates there, but also the VIX just went haywire for a period of 5 hours or so. It got to the 50s

insights.claim: Patel says Nomura can distribute unique structured-product risk into its extensive Japan retail/institutional distribution and source liquidity there that supports its global businesses.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Japan distribution is a two-way edge: it both warehouses risk and provides differentiated liquidity that global desks can monetize.
insights.quote: there's different types of risks or structure products that we can distribute into our, you know, very uh extensive kind of Japan distribution.

themes.theme: Product Mix Shift and QIS Expansion

insights.claim: Patel says Nomura's QIS franchise was historically fixed-income-centric and Europe-based, but over the past three years it diversified significantly into equity strategies, hired a US team, and built Japan distribution.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Equity QIS is a growing growth engine; clients seeking customized systematic strategies have a new diversified provider.
insights.quote: Over the past three years, we've diversified quite significantly into equity products and strategies. We've hired a team in the US. We've hired distribution teams in Japan

insights.claim: Patel states that five years ago Nomura was overweight macro and flow businesses (equity derivatives, FX, rates) and overweight hedge-fund clients, and has since deliberately diversified into insurers, sponsors, corporates, and asset managers plus bespoke financing/solutions.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: A deliberate client-base diversification away from hedge funds toward stickier real-money and corporate flow reduces revenue cyclicality.
insights.quote: five years ago we were probably overweight um the macro and flow businesses... and probably overweight our hedge fund client base

insights.claim: Patel notes Nomura had FX product gaps it has been filling, being large in Asia and Europe but more limited in the US, and rebuilt its European equities business about four to five years ago.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Identifies specific regional product build-outs (US FX, European equities) where Nomura is newly scaling capacity for clients.
insights.quote: in FX we're very large in Asia and Europe more limited in the US we've built that out Europe we rebuilt our our equities business there uh four or five years ago

themes.theme: Talent: Recruiting Philosophy and Junior Attrition Risk

insights.claim: Patel says he now screens recruits for aptitude, effort, attitude, and increasingly passion, because the bar on aptitude has risen so high that nearly all candidates are qualified, making genuine long-term interest in markets the key differentiator.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: A hiring lens applicable across the industry as commoditized technical skill forces firms to select on durable motivation.
insights.quote: The three things I look for are aptitude, effort, and attitude. And the new one I would add, I would say, is passion.

insights.claim: Patel flags rising attrition of analysts and associates three to five years into their careers who are leaving the financial industry entirely rather than for competitors, calling it a street-wide problem.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: A structural human-capital risk for sell-side firms: training investment leaks out of the industry, raising the value of retention and passion-screening.
insights.quote: We're seeing much higher attrition rates of of let's say analysts and associates three, four, five years out of the business and they're not leaving for competitors. They're actually leaving the industry.

insights.claim: Patel says he specifically values candidates with military backgrounds and student-athletes for their discipline and proven ability to balance heavy workloads, despite military applicants often entering finance later at 24-26.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: A non-obvious recruiting signal preference that points to where firms increasingly hunt for differentiated junior talent.
insights.quote: anyone with a military background... so impressive in so many ways

insights.claim: Patel says Nomura's culture centers on bottom-line P&L over sales credits, and a new-hire survey ranks the culture between sales, trading, and research as the number-one improvement over their prior firm every single year.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that intra-desk alignment is a recruiting and retention asset, and that bottom-line orientation can be measured and reinforced.
insights.quote: out of a list of seven or eight things, I think number one, every single year has been the culture between sales, trading, and research.

insights.claim: Patel quotes the principle 'sales credits only matter if there's sales credibility,' and says Nomura regularly reviews and adjusts sales-credit methodology with both sales and trading signing off so reported producer numbers track actual desk P&L.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: A governance mechanism to keep incentive metrics honest, ensuring management allocates pay and resources based on real economics, not gamed credits.
insights.quote: my boss has said it to me, you know, sales credits only matter if there's sales credibility.

insights.claim: Patel argues traders 10-15 years ago prized risk P&L (total P&L less sales credits paid), which created structural tension to underpay sales credits, but the shift to pure bottom-line P&L removes that incentive conflict.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Explains the mechanism behind historical sales-trading friction and how redefining the trader scorecard resolves it, improving client experience.
insights.quote: they probably viewed their most... important measure of uh performance as their risk P&L, which would be their total P&L less their sales credits paid. And that's totally changed.

insights.claim: Patel observes that clients now expect consistency of risk appetite and pricing within a single product but rarely demand cross-product subsidization (e.g., paying up in equity derivatives to get aggressive rates pricing).
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Tells desks that within-product consistency matters most for client experience, while cross-product horse-trading has largely faded.
insights.quote: very rarely do we see clients now asking for more risk capital or or aggressiveness and balance sheet pricing because they're important in another product.

themes.theme: Outlook: Record Revenues and the Non-Bank Liquidity Threat

insights.claim: Patel says last year was an all-time record for Nomura's global markets business, attributing market-share gains over the past three years to management's investment in sales and the client franchise alongside strong markets.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Confirms Nomura's markets franchise is in a genuine up-cycle, validating its strategic pivot for investors tracking the firm.
insights.quote: Last year was a all-time record for us in the global markets business and uh we're quite excited about the opportunity ahead of us.

insights.claim: Patel identifies non-bank liquidity providers reporting huge revenue numbers and expanding into more products, regions, and OTC businesses as the most significant change in the competitive landscape.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Signals structural margin and share pressure on traditional dealers as electronic market-makers push from listed into OTC territory.
insights.quote: when you look at the non-bank liquidity providers... these are just huge numbers and you're seeing them expand into more and more products and more and more regions

insights.claim: Patel's guiding lesson from a 28-year career is to 'expect the unexpected'—across market shocks, people departures, client failures or launches, regulatory shifts, and technology—and he cites AI as a potential large accelerant of business change.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: A risk-management posture emphasizing optionality and adaptability, with AI singled out as the next major disruptor of the markets business model.
insights.quote: the number one lesson especially in financial markets is to expect the unexpected

insights.claim: Patel says Nomura uses AI for KYC onboarding, credit reviews, and straight-through processing of structured notes (auto-reading inquiries, pricing, and generating term sheets), and is further along on STP than on scraping Bloomberg chats and phone calls.
insights.speaker: Samir Patel
insights.so_what: Concrete view of where AI is actually delivering sell-side efficiency today (STP, KYC) versus still aspirational (unstructured comms capture).
insights.quote: we have tools that can read inquiries automatically price it. We get all the pricing back to clients and then do the term sheet.
